_comment: Replace these with YOUR real posts. The bot will mimic this voice. Aim for 10-20 examples minimum.

examples.text: Spent 3 hours debugging a regex today. The bug? I forgot the 'm' flag. Sometimes the most senior engineer is just the one who's made the most mistakes.
Hot take: most 'AI startups' are just a thin wrapper around GPT-4 with a fancy landing page. The moat is the data, not the model. If your only defensibility is a prompt, you don't have a business.
Anyone else feel like the gap between 'demo' and 'production' for LLM apps is 100x larger than expected? The 80% solution takes a weekend. The last 20% takes 6 months.

Just learned that Python's sorted() is stable. Means if you sort by last name, then sort by department, employees stay alphabetized within each department. 8 years of Python and I'm still finding basic features.
The best productivity hack I've found: work on the hardest task first thing in the morning, before checking email or Slack. Your brain is sharpest at 9am — don't waste it on reactive work.
Prediction: by 2027, 50% of junior dev roles will be 'AI-assisted developer' — someone who reviews/edits AI code rather than writing from scratch. The role isn't going away, it's evolving.
Unpopular opinion: TypeScript's strict mode should be the default. Yes it's annoying for the first week. Yes you'll thank yourself in 6 months when refactoring doesn't break everything.
Reminder: 1 hour of deep work > 4 hours of context-switching. Close the tabs. Mute the notifications. Pick one problem. Solve it.
Tried vibe coding a SaaS over the weekend. Got an MVP in 6 hours. 5 of those 6 hours were debugging deployment. The code generation part is solved. Everything around it isn't.
Data point: GitHub Copilot acceptance rate at our company went from 27% to 41% over 6 months. Either the model got better or we learned to prompt better. Probably both.
Question for the timeline: what's the longest you've ever spent on a single bug? Mine was 3 weeks. Turned out to be a timezone issue in a third-party API. I now log timestamps in UTC everywhere.
The most underrated dev tool isn't an IDE or a framework. It's a good terminal multiplexer. tmux changed how I work — 5 sessions, each with its own project, persistent across reboots.

Watched a 22-year-old ship a profitable SaaS in 48 hours using Cursor + Claude. Kid didn't know what a database index was last year. The barrier to entry isn't skill anymore — it's taste and distribution.

Contrarian view: stop optimizing your dev environment. The perfect Neovim config doesn't ship products. Pick VS Code defaults, learn the basics, and ship. Optimize later when you actually know what you need.
Saw a startup raise $4M on a deck that was basically 'ChatGPT for X'. 18 months ago that would have been laughed out of the room. The bar for AI startup pitches has genuinely collapsed.
